ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef noodle soup class (FOODON_00002473); no specific class exists for pickled cabbage and tripe variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers noodle soup with meat as a composite dish; no specific code for pickled cabbage beef tripe noodle soup. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific composite noodle soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of pickled cabbage sliced beef and tripe noodle soup weighing approximately 550 g, comprising noodles, broth, beef slices, tripe, and pickled cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup (composite dish) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables, with component-level estimation for pickled cabbage beef tripe noodle soup
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for pickled cabbage sliced beef and tripe noodle soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values estimated by component-level analysis: wheat noodles (USDA SR Legacy #20109), cooked beef (USDA SR Legacy #13365), beef tripe (USDA SR Legacy #13356), pickled cabbage/sauerkraut (USDA SR Legacy #11439), and beef broth (USDA SR Legacy #06170). Proportions estimated as approximately 35% noodles, 15% beef slices, 10% tripe, 10% pickled cabbage, 30% broth by weight. Values represent per-100g of the assembled dish as served.

gi_source.title: Noodle soup (wheat noodles, cooked) — GI analogous estimate based on wheat noodle soup entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pickled cabbage sliced beef and tripe noodle soup. GI estimated at 52 based on wheat noodle soup analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., instant noodle soup GI ~52, boiled wheat noodles GI ~47–58 range). The presence of protein from beef and tripe, fat, and acidic pickled cabbage (acidity lowers GI) supports a low-to-medium GI estimate; 52 is used as a conservative mid-point. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 59.4 g available carbohydrate per 550 g serving) / 100 = 30.9, rounded to 30.5 accounting for fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth, pickled cabbage, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 550 g bowl provides approximately 2,310 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit the soup liquid consumed, and avoid adding extra soy sauce or chilli condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein, fat, and acidic pickled cabbage
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 52 (low category) and GL of approximately 30 per bowl indicate a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion of noodles, increase the proportion of beef and vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the high-sodium broth also warrants attention for cardiometabolic risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, saturated fat from beef and tripe, and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~2,310 mg per bowl), saturated fat (~6 g per bowl), and cholesterol (~209 mg per bowl) from beef and tripe makes this dish a high-concern food for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be infrequent, portion-controlled, and paired with a request for leaner cuts and less broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pickled Cabbage Sliced Beef and Tripe Noodle Soup (酸菜牛肉肚丝面汤, suān cài niúròu dù sī miàn tāng) is a Chinese noodle soup dish rooted in the Lanzhou beef noodle tradition of Gansu Province, China. The dish features hand-pulled or machine-cut wheat noodles served in a rich, slow-simmered beef bone broth, topped with thinly sliced cooked beef, tender strips of beef tripe (honeycomb or smooth), and a generous portion of suan cai (酸菜), a fermented and pickled cabbage that imparts a characteristic sour, tangy flavour. The broth is typically seasoned with soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and chilli oil, giving it a savoury, mildly spicy, and umami-rich profile. The noodles are chewy and absorb the broth well, while the tripe provides a slightly gelatinous, firm texture contrasting with the tender beef slices. The pickled cabbage adds acidity and crunch, balancing the richness of the broth and meat. In Singapore, this dish is served at Lanzhou-style noodle restaurants and is priced at the premium end of noodle soups (~SGD 19). Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate source of protein (~40 g per bowl from beef and tripe), provides B12, iron, and zinc from the animal proteins, but is high in sodium (~2,310 mg per bowl) due to the broth and pickled vegetables, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of 52 places it in the low GI category, partially due to the acidic pickled cabbage and the protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying. Vitamin D is not reported in the component sources used for estimation.
common_user_queries: What is pickled cabbage beef noodle soup?
Is suan cai beef noodle soup healthy?
How much sodium is in beef noodle soup?

Can diabetics eat beef noodle soup?
What is tripe in noodle soup?
Is pickled cabbage noodle soup gluten free?
How many calories in a bowl of beef tripe noodle soup?
Is beef tripe noodle soup suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by component-level analysis using USDA SR Legacy entries for wheat noodles, cooked beef, beef tripe, pickled cabbage, and beef broth with estimated proportions. GI was estimated by analogy from wheat noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the proportions are consistent with standard Lanzhou-style noodle soup recipes.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for estimation. All other micronutrient values are component-level estimates and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or an exact dish entry if one becomes available in HPB Singapore Foodo or an equivalent approved regional database. Sodium content is particularly variable depending on the amount of broth consumed and the restaurant's seasoning practices; the value of 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,310 mg per 550 g bowl) represents a typical estimate. The halal status is marked false as this dish contains beef tripe and the halal certification of the specific restaurant (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles) was not confirmed at time of processing. GI set to 52 based on analogous wheat noodle soup entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; the acidic pickled cabbage component is expected to lower the effective GI relative to plain wheat noodles (GI ~47–58). GL per serving calculated as (52 × 59.4 g available carbohydrate in 550 g serving) / 100 ≈ 30.9, reported as 30.5 after fibre adjustment.
